title: Net Unrealized Appreciation (NUA) Tax Calculator
summary: Calculates the total tax liability for Net Unrealized Appreciation (NUA) of employer stock from a qualified retirement plan, applying capital gains tax to NUA and ordinary income tax to the cost basis.
selection_hints: Ordinary income tax on cost basis
applicable_when: Calculating tax on appreciated employer stock from a qualified retirement plan.
Distributing employer stock in a lump sum from a 401(k) or similar plan.
Seeking favorable capital gains tax rates on the appreciated portion of employer stock.
The distribution meets NUA eligibility requirements.
not_applicable_when: The employer stock distribution does not qualify for NUA treatment (e.g., not a lump sum, no qualifying event).
Rolling employer stock into an IRA (NUA rules generally don't apply).
Calculating general capital gains on stock held in a regular brokerage account.
The stock value is less than its cost basis (indicating a loss, not appreciation).
Only seeking to calculate tax on ordinary retirement plan distributions.
scope_boundaries: Applies only to employer stock from qualified retirement plans.
Calculates tax for the NUA portion and the cost basis separately.
Assumes the distribution qualifies for NUA treatment.
Requires market value, cost basis, capital gains rate, and income tax rate inputs.

common_confusions.label: NUA Eligibility
common_confusions.difference: This calculation assumes NUA eligibility criteria are met (e.g., lump-sum distribution, qualifying event). If conditions are not met, the entire distribution may be taxed as ordinary income or rolled over.

common_confusions.label: NUA vs. Regular Retirement Distribution Tax
common_confusions.difference: NUA provides a specific tax advantage for employer stock, taxing appreciation at capital gains rates. Regular retirement distributions are typically taxed entirely as ordinary income.

common_confusions.label: NUA vs. Stock Sale in Brokerage Account
common_confusions.difference: NUA applies specifically to employer stock from qualified retirement plans upon distribution. Stock sold in a regular brokerage account is subject to standard capital gains rules.

title: Implied Volatility Calculation (Black-Scholes Model)
summary: Computes the implied volatility of a European call or put option by inverting the Black-Scholes option pricing model, given its market price and other option parameters.
applicable_when: Determining the implied volatility of a European option.
You have the market price of a European call or put option.
Using the Black-Scholes option pricing framework.
Analyzing option contracts where dividends are not explicitly modeled.
not_applicable_when: Pricing American options (due to early exercise possibility).
Options on assets with discrete dividends (without dividend adjustments).
When using models other than Black-Scholes (e.g., binomial tree, Monte Carlo).
For exotic options with complex payoffs.
scope_boundaries: Requires market price, stock price, strike price, time to expiration (in years), and risk-free rate.
Option type ('call' or 'put') can be specified; 'call' is the default.
Input values must be positive, and the risk-free rate should be within a plausible range (e.g., -10% to 100%).
Calculates implied volatility as a percentage.
Assumes a continuously compounded risk-free rate.

common_confusions.label: European vs. American Options
common_confusions.difference: This model is for European options only, which can only be exercised at expiration, unlike American options which can be exercised any time up to expiration.

common_confusions.label: Call vs. Put Option
common_confusions.difference: Specify 'call' or 'put' as the option type; the default is 'call' if not provided.

common_confusions.label: Continuously vs. Annually Compounded Rate
common_confusions.difference: The Black-Scholes model uses a continuously compounded risk-free rate. If an annually compounded rate is provided, it must be converted.

common_confusions.label: Implied vs. Historical Volatility
common_confusions.difference: Implied volatility is derived from the option's market price, reflecting market expectations of future volatility, whereas historical volatility is based on past price movements.

title: Out-of-Pocket Cost Calculation for Healthcare
summary: Calculates the policyholder's total out-of-pocket cost for a healthcare service, considering their deductible, coinsurance, and the annual out-of-pocket maximum.
applicable_when: Estimating personal healthcare costs for a specific medical service.
Determining the remaining balance for a medical bill after insurance.
Understanding the financial impact of deductibles, coinsurance, and out-of-pocket maximums.
Assessing how close one is to reaching their annual out-of-pocket limit.
not_applicable_when: Calculating health insurance premiums.
Determining costs for services not covered by insurance.
Estimating costs for out-of-network healthcare providers.
Calculating total household medical expenses across multiple individuals with separate out-of-pocket limits.
When only a fixed copayment applies without deductible or coinsurance.
scope_boundaries: Calculates costs for a single healthcare event or aggregated services contributing to a single out-of-pocket calculation.
Assumes all billed services are covered by the health insurance plan.
Assumes all healthcare services are provided by in-network providers.
Requires inputs for total billed amount, deductible, coinsurance rate, and out-of-pocket maximum.
Applies to individual out-of-pocket limits.

common_confusions.label: Premiums
common_confusions.difference: This card calculates cost-sharing for services, not the regular monthly or annual premiums paid to maintain insurance coverage.

common_confusions.label: Copayments
common_confusions.difference: While copayments often count towards the out-of-pocket maximum, this calculation focuses on deductible and coinsurance. If only a copay applies, this card might not be the most direct fit.

common_confusions.label: Out-of-network costs
common_confusions.difference: This calculation primarily applies to in-network services. Out-of-network costs typically do not count towards your in-network out-of-pocket maximum.

common_confusions.label: Non-covered services
common_confusions.difference: Costs for services not covered by your health insurance plan do not count towards the out-of-pocket maximum and are not directly calculated here.

common_confusions.label: Total Annual Healthcare Spending
common_confusions.difference: This card calculates the out-of-pocket cost for a specific service or event, not your total annual healthcare spending, which might include premiums or non-covered expenses.

title: Nanny Tax
summary: Calculates the total federal nanny tax liability, encompassing both employer and employee shares of Social Security and Medicare taxes, plus federal unemployment tax (FUTA), based on annual wages paid to a household employee.
applicable_when: Calculating federal tax liability for household employees
Hiring a nanny, babysitter, or other domestic worker
Determining employer and employee share of Social Security and Medicare taxes for household wages
Calculating Federal Unemployment Tax (FUTA) for household employees
When total cash wages paid to a household employee exceed federal thresholds
not_applicable_when: Employee is a spouse, parent, or child under 18 (not primarily working as household help)
Household help hired through an employment agency
Calculating only state-level nanny or household employment taxes
Determining taxes for independent contractors (not household employees)
scope_boundaries: Focuses exclusively on federal nanny tax components: Social Security, Medicare, and FUTA
Calculates both employer and employee shares of Social Security and Medicare taxes
Does not include state or local employment taxes
Assumes the individual is classified as a household employee, not an independent contractor
Based on annual wages paid to a single household employee

common_confusions.label: Federal vs. State Nanny Tax
common_confusions.difference: This calculation covers federal Social Security, Medicare, and FUTA taxes only; it does not include any state or local nanny taxes.

common_confusions.label: Household Employee vs. Independent Contractor
common_confusions.difference: This tax applies only to household employees as defined by the IRS, not to independent contractors.

common_confusions.label: Nanny Tax Applicability Thresholds
common_confusions.difference: The tax is generally only applicable if cash wages meet specific annual thresholds (e.g., $2,300 for Social Security and Medicare); this card calculates the tax amount given rates and wages, not applicability based on thresholds.

title: Hindenburg Omen
summary: Analyzes market conditions to determine if the Hindenburg Omen signal is active, indicating a heightened probability of a stock market downturn or crash. It evaluates specific criteria related to 52-week highs/lows, market trend, and the McClellan Oscillator.
applicable_when: When seeking a technical signal for potential stock market crashes.
When analyzing market breadth and momentum indicators like 52-week highs/lows and McClellan Oscillator.
When the market is in an uptrend but showing signs of internal weakness.
When evaluating short-term market risk for approximately 30 days.
not_applicable_when: When analyzing individual stock performance or specific sectors.
When seeking long-term investment strategies or fundamental analysis.
When the market does not exhibit simultaneous high numbers of new 52-week highs and lows.
When a market uptrend is not present or the McClellan Oscillator is positive.
scope_boundaries: Focuses on the broad stock market index, not individual securities.
Primarily a short-term signal, relevant for about 30 trading days.
Considers specific criteria: percentage of 52-week highs/lows, market index uptrend, and McClellan Oscillator value.
Designed to predict increased probability of a downturn, not a guaranteed outcome.

common_confusions.label: Guaranteed market crash prediction
common_confusions.difference: The Hindenburg Omen is an indicator of increased probability for a downturn, not a certainty, and has a history of false alarms.

common_confusions.label: Individual stock indicator
common_confusions.difference: This omen applies to the overall stock market or a broad market index, not to the performance of single stocks.

common_confusions.label: Long-term market outlook
common_confusions.difference: The signal is generally considered relevant for a short period, typically around 30 trading days, and does not provide long-term forecasts.

title: Cash Flow from Operating Activities (CFO)
summary: Calculates Cash Flow from Operating Activities (CFO) using the indirect method. It adjusts net income for non-cash expenses like depreciation and amortization, tax adjustments, and changes in working capital components such as accounts receivable, liabilities, and inventories, to determine the cash generated by a company's core business operations.
applicable_when: Calculating cash generated by core business operations.
Analyzing a company's operational efficiency and liquidity.
Using the indirect method to prepare a cash flow statement.
Adjusting net income from an accrual basis to a cash basis for operating activities.
Evaluating a company's ability to generate cash internally.
not_applicable_when: Calculating cash flow using the direct method.
Analyzing cash flows from investing activities.
Analyzing cash flows from financing activities.
When only interested in net income (accrual basis profit).
scope_boundaries: Includes: Net income, depreciation, amortization, tax adjustments, changes in accounts receivable, changes in liabilities, changes in inventories, changes in other operating activities.
Excludes: Cash flows from investing activities (e.g., purchase/sale of assets), cash flows from financing activities (e.g., debt, equity, dividends).
Method: Specifically uses the indirect method.
Focus: Core, ongoing business operations.

common_confusions.label: Direct vs. Indirect Method
common_confusions.difference: This formula specifically applies the indirect method, starting with net income. It does not calculate CFO using the direct method which tracks individual cash receipts and payments.

common_confusions.label: Input convention for working capital changes
common_confusions.difference: The inputs for working capital changes (e.g., 'changes_in_accounts_receivable') are expected to represent the direct cash flow impact (e.g., a $15,000 increase in AR is entered as -15,000). If raw period-over-period changes are provided, the calculation will be incorrect without adjustment.

common_confusions.label: CFO vs. Funds From Operations (FFO)
common_confusions.difference: Funds From Operations (FFO) adjusts net income for non-cash expenses, but CFO further incorporates changes in working capital, providing a more complete picture of operating cash.

common_confusions.label: CFO vs. Net Income
common_confusions.difference: Net Income is an accrual-based measure of profitability, while CFO represents actual cash generated from operations, which can differ significantly due to non-cash items and working capital changes.

title: Earnings Per Share (EPS) Growth Rate
summary: Calculates the percentage change in a company's Earnings Per Share (EPS) from a previous period to the current period, indicating its growth or decline.
applicable_when: Assessing a company's profitability trend over time.
Evaluating the growth or decline in earnings available to each share.
Comparing the financial performance of a company between two specific periods.
Analyzing how efficiently a company converts revenue into per-share profit.
not_applicable_when: You need the actual Price/Earnings-to-Growth (PEG) Ratio.
You only have EPS data for a single period.
You are looking for absolute EPS values rather than a rate of change.
You need to calculate compound annual growth over multiple periods.
You are interested in overall company revenue growth, not per-share earnings.
scope_boundaries: Requires two distinct Earnings Per Share (EPS) values: one for the current period and one for a previous period.
Outputs a percentage value representing the growth or decline.
Focuses strictly on earnings on a per-share basis.
Applies to financial analysis of publicly traded companies or businesses with defined share structures.

common_confusions.label: Price/Earnings-to-Growth (PEG) Ratio
common_confusions.difference: The PEG Ratio compares a company's P/E to its expected EPS growth to assess valuation. This card calculates only the direct period-over-period percentage change in EPS, not the PEG ratio.

common_confusions.label: Earnings Per Share (EPS)
common_confusions.difference: This card computes the *growth rate* of EPS between two periods, not the raw EPS value for a single period.

common_confusions.label: Net Income Growth Rate
common_confusions.difference: This calculation is specifically for *per-share* earnings growth. Net income growth rate measures the growth of total company profit.

common_confusions.label: Compound Annual Growth Rate (CAGR)
common_confusions.difference: This formula calculates simple period-over-period growth. CAGR is used for smoothed average annual growth over multiple years.

title: Income Annuity
summary: Calculates the fixed monthly payment amount for an immediate annuity given a lump sum premium, annual interest rate, and payout duration in years.
applicable_when: You need to determine the fixed monthly income from a lump sum immediate annuity.
You are planning retirement income streams from a one-time premium.
You want to calculate a guaranteed periodic cash flow over a set period.
not_applicable_when: You are looking for payments from a deferred annuity (payments start later).
You need to calculate payments for an annuity with variable or investment-linked returns.
You are calculating the total future value or accumulated sum of an annuity.
You are interested in the total return of an annuity.
scope_boundaries: Calculates fixed monthly payments only.
Assumes payments are made at the end of each period by default.
Requires a lump sum premium, annual interest rate, and payout duration in years.
Does not account for inflation adjustments to payments.

common_confusions.label: Immediate Annuity vs. Deferred Annuity
common_confusions.difference: This card calculates for an *immediate* annuity, where payments begin soon after the lump sum premium is paid. It is not for *deferred* annuities, where payments start at a future date.

common_confusions.label: Fixed Annuity vs. Variable Annuity
common_confusions.difference: This card calculates a *fixed* monthly payment. It does not account for variable annuities where payments fluctuate based on underlying investment performance.

common_confusions.label: Annuity Payment Timing (End vs. Beginning of Period)
common_confusions.difference: By default, this calculation assumes payments are made at the *end* of each month (ordinary annuity). Payments made at the *beginning* of the month (annuity due) would result in a slightly different payment amount.

title: Bell Curve Data Percentage within One Standard Deviation
summary: Calculates the actual percentage of provided data points that fall within one standard deviation from the mean in a given dataset. This card applies the concept of the empirical rule for normal distributions to concrete data.
selection_hints: Data point analysis relative to mean
applicable_when: You have a dataset and its mean/standard deviation.
You need to know the proportion of data points falling within one standard deviation of the mean.
You are working with data assumed to be normally distributed or want to test its adherence to the empirical rule for one standard deviation.
Analyzing financial security returns or market sensitivity where normal distribution is a factor.
Assessing the spread of performance metrics or test scores.
not_applicable_when: Your data is definitively not normally distributed, as the 'bell curve' interpretation won't hold.
You only need the theoretical 68% from the empirical rule without checking actual data.
You need to calculate percentages for two or three standard deviations, not just one.
You do not have a list of actual data points, only summary statistics (mean, std dev).
You are looking for probabilities of specific values, rather than the percentage of actual observations within a range.
scope_boundaries: Calculates percentage for *one* standard deviation only.
Requires 'mean', 'standard deviation', and a list of 'data_points' as inputs.
The output is the exact calculated percentage from the provided data, not the theoretical 68%.
Results are rounded to two decimal places.
Interpretation as a 'bell curve' is most appropriate for normally distributed data.

common_confusions.label: Empirical Rule (68-95-99.7 Rule)
common_confusions.difference: This card *calculates* the actual percentage of data within one standard deviation based on your specific dataset, whereas the Empirical Rule *states* that 68% of data *should* fall within one standard deviation for a perfectly normal distribution.

common_confusions.label: Percentage within Two or Three Standard Deviations
common_confusions.difference: This card only calculates the percentage of data points within *one* standard deviation. Separate calculations are needed for two or three standard deviations.

common_confusions.label: Theoretical Normal Distribution Probability
common_confusions.difference: This card quantifies the actual distribution of *your specific dataset*. It does not calculate theoretical probabilities using a probability density function for a perfectly normal distribution.

common_confusions.label: Chebyshev's Inequality
common_confusions.difference: Chebyshev's Inequality provides a *lower bound* for any distribution. This card applies specifically to assessing characteristics of (assumed) normal distributions, providing an *exact calculated percentage* for 1 standard deviation from your data.

title: TFSA Over-Contribution Tax Penalty Calculation
summary: Calculates the monthly tax penalty for over-contributions to a Canadian Tax-Free Savings Account (TFSA) based on annual limits.
applicable_when: Calculating tax penalties for exceeding TFSA contribution limits.
Determining penalties for Canadian Tax-Free Savings Account over-contributions.
Assessing financial consequences of TFSA overages in Canada.
Reviewing past TFSA contributions against annual limits for penalty assessment.
not_applicable_when: Calculating general TFSA contribution room or limits without over-contributions.
Determining penalties for other types of registered accounts (e.g., RRSP, RESP).
Evaluating investment accounts outside of Canada.
Seeking general information on TFSA benefits or investment strategies.
No TFSA over-contributions have occurred.
scope_boundaries: Applies specifically to Canadian Tax-Free Savings Accounts (TFSA).
Calculates a 1% monthly penalty on over-contributed amounts.
Requires annual contribution data and corresponding annual TFSA limits as inputs.
Focuses solely on tax penalty calculation, not general TFSA account management.
Does not automatically factor in unused contribution room carried forward from previous years unless specified.

common_confusions.label: Penalty duration (full year vs. actual)
common_confusions.difference: This card assumes penalties accrue for a full year for any year an over-contribution occurred, not based on the actual duration the over-contribution existed before withdrawal.

common_confusions.label: TFSA contribution room (annual vs. carry-forward)
common_confusions.difference: This card calculates over-contributions based on the annual limit only, without automatically including unused contribution room carried forward from previous years.

common_confusions.label: Contribution types (all inclusive vs. specific exclusions)
common_confusions.difference: This card considers all provided contributions as counting towards the annual limit. It does not automatically exclude qualifying transfers or exempt contributions.

title: Long Straddle Profit Calculation
summary: Calculates the potential profit or loss for a long straddle options strategy based on the underlying asset's price movement direction (up or down) at expiration. It accounts for the asset's expiration price, the common strike price, and the total net premium paid for both options.
applicable_when: Anticipating significant price movement (up or down) of an underlying asset
Expecting high volatility in the market due to an imminent event (e.g., earnings report, news)
Aiming to profit from a strong move in either direction by the underlying asset
When a trader believes the underlying asset will move from a low volatility state to a high volatility state
not_applicable_when: Anticipating the underlying asset's price to remain stable or move minimally
When the underlying asset's price is expected to be exactly at the strike price at expiration (maximum loss scenario)
When seeking profits from low volatility or time decay
If the market fails to react strongly enough to an anticipated event
scope_boundaries: Calculates profit/loss on a per-share basis by default.
Requires knowing the underlying asset's price at expiration.
Assumes the same strike price and expiration date for both the call and put options.
Considers the combined net premium paid for both the call and put options.
Excludes trading commissions from profit calculation by default.

common_confusions.label: Profit calculation unit (per share vs. per contract)
common_confusions.difference: This formula defaults to per-share profit calculation. It can be adjusted to per-contract (100 shares) if explicitly specified, which will multiply the result by 100.

common_confusions.label: Inclusion of trading commissions
common_confusions.difference: The calculation by default excludes trading commissions. To include commissions, an additional input for 'commission_per_share' would be required and subtracted from the profit.

common_confusions.label: Long Straddle vs. Short Straddle
common_confusions.difference: A Long Straddle profits from large price movements in either direction (volatility increase), while a Short Straddle profits from minimal price movement and time decay (volatility decrease), aiming for the underlying asset to stay near the strike price.

title: Covered Interest Rate Parity

Analyzing currency markets under a no-arbitrage assumption.
Evaluating covered foreign exchange transactions.
Understanding the relationship between spot rates, forward rates, and interest rate differentials.
When hedging foreign exchange risk using forward contracts.
not_applicable_when: Forecasting future spot exchange rates without using forward contracts.
Seeking to identify and exploit actual arbitrage opportunities.
When foreign exchange risk is not covered or hedged.
When dealing with uncovered interest rate parity.
scope_boundaries: Assumes a perfect capital market with no transaction costs.
Applies a no-arbitrage condition for currency and interest rate markets.
Requires spot exchange rate, domestic, and foreign interest rates as inputs.
Focuses on the relationship involving forward contracts.
Interest rates are annual and should be provided as decimals.

common_confusions.label: Uncovered Interest Rate Parity (UIP)
common_confusions.difference: CIP explicitly involves using forward contracts to hedge foreign exchange risk and assumes no arbitrage. UIP forecasts future spot rates without hedging and does not use forward contracts.

title: Compound Interest
summary: Calculates the total compound interest earned or paid on an initial principal amount over a specified period, considering a fixed compounding frequency.
applicable_when: You need to calculate interest on an investment where interest is added to the principal periodically.
Analyzing the growth of a savings account, fixed deposit, or bond.
Determining the total interest paid on a loan with periodic compounding.
Comparing investment options based on their compounding frequency.
not_applicable_when: You are looking for simple interest, which does not compound.
Interest is compounded continuously (i.e., infinite compounding periods).
The interest rate or principal amount changes over the investment term.
You need to calculate the future value or total amount, not just the interest.
scope_boundaries: Assumes a constant annual interest rate.
Assumes a fixed principal amount without additional contributions or withdrawals.
Requires a specific, discrete compounding frequency (e.g., annually, semi-annually, monthly, quarterly).
Input 'rate' is an annual rate expressed as a decimal (e.g., 0.05 for 5%).
Input 'time' is in years.

common_confusions.label: Simple Interest
common_confusions.difference: Unlike simple interest, which is only on the principal, compound interest is calculated on both the initial principal and the accumulated interest from previous periods.

common_confusions.label: Continuous Compounding
common_confusions.difference: This formula applies to discrete compounding periods (e.g., daily, monthly, annually), not continuous compounding, which involves an infinite number of compounding periods.

common_confusions.label: Future Value
common_confusions.difference: This formula outputs only the *interest earned*. If you need the *total amount* at the end of the period (principal + interest), use a Future Value formula.

common_confusions.label: Effective Annual Rate (EAR)
common_confusions.difference: EAR represents the actual annual rate after accounting for compounding. This formula calculates interest using the nominal annual rate and compounding frequency.

title: Tax Benefit Calculation
summary: Calculates the final tax liability after applying a tax credit, differentiating between refundable and nonrefundable credits.
applicable_when: You need to determine the remaining tax liability after applying a credit.
You want to understand the impact of a refundable versus nonrefundable tax credit.
You are calculating the net tax position, including potential refunds.
not_applicable_when: You are calculating tax deductions, exemptions, or other non-credit tax benefits.
You need to determine the initial tax liability itself, not its reduction by a credit.
You are looking for general tax planning advice beyond credit application.
scope_boundaries: Focuses specifically on the application of tax credits to reduce tax liability.
Accounts for whether a credit can generate a refund (refundable) or only reduce liability to zero (nonrefundable).
Assumes the initial tax liability and credit amount are known.

common_confusions.label: Tax Credit vs. Tax Deduction
common_confusions.difference: This card calculates tax credits (direct reduction of tax owed), not tax deductions (which reduce taxable income). A tax credit directly lowers the amount of tax you owe, while a deduction reduces the income on which your tax is calculated.

common_confusions.label: Net Tax Position vs. Minimum Zero Owed
common_confusions.difference: The result can be a negative value, indicating a refund if the credit is refundable and exceeds the liability. If you only want to see the amount still owed (a minimum of zero), a different calculation is needed for output interpretation.

title: Zero-Volatility Spread (Z-spread)
summary: Calculates the zero-volatility spread (Z-spread) of a bond, which is the constant spread added to the Treasury spot rates that makes the present value of a bond's cash flows equal to its current market price. It quantifies the credit risk premium.
applicable_when: You need to assess the credit risk premium of a bond.
You want to compare a bond's yield to the entire Treasury spot rate curve, not just a single benchmark.
The question explicitly asks for the 'Z-spread' or 'zero-volatility spread'.
You are evaluating a bond without embedded options.
not_applicable_when: You need to account for embedded options (e.g., call/put features) and interest rate volatility.
You only need a simple spread over a single benchmark government bond (e.g., G-spread).
You are interested in a single discount rate like Yield to Maturity (YTM) for a simple comparison.
The instrument is not a fixed-income bond.
scope_boundaries: Requires bond price, all future cash flows, corresponding Treasury spot rates, and the periods to each cash flow.
Calculates a single, constant spread applicable across the entire Treasury spot rate curve.
Assumes the bond has no embedded options (e.g., callable, putable).

common_confusions.label: Option-Adjusted Spread (OAS)
common_confusions.difference: Z-spread assumes no embedded options and a static interest rate environment, while OAS accounts for the impact of embedded options and potential interest rate volatility.

common_confusions.label: Yield to Maturity (YTM)
common_confusions.difference: YTM is a single discount rate that equates a bond's price to its cash flows, whereas Z-spread is a constant spread added to each point on the Treasury spot rate curve.

common_confusions.label: G-Spread (Government Spread)
common_confusions.difference: G-spread is the spread of a bond's yield over a specific benchmark government bond (e.g., a Treasury of similar maturity), whereas Z-spread is the spread over the entire government spot rate curve.

applicable_when: Comparing the tax impact of itemized deductions vs. standard deduction.
Evaluating the tax benefit of mortgage interest and other itemized deductions.
When itemized deductions exceed the standard deduction.
For federal income tax calculations related to homeownership.

When claiming the standard deduction and not itemizing.

Assumes itemized deductions are provided after applying all relevant tax law limits (e.g., mortgage principal limits).

title: Median
summary: Calculates the central value of a sorted list of numbers. It identifies the midpoint of a dataset, useful for understanding data distribution, especially when outliers may distort the average.
applicable_when: Determining the midpoint of a dataset.
Analyzing data with potential outliers or skewed distributions.
Evaluating investment performance or return rates.
Needing a measure of central tendency robust to extreme values.
Comparing against mean or mode for a complete statistical overview.
not_applicable_when: The arithmetic average (mean) is specifically required.
The most frequent value (mode) is needed.
The input list of numbers is empty.
Analyzing data for sum or cumulative effects.
scope_boundaries: Input must be a list of numerical values.
Each element in the input list must be an integer or float.
Output is a single numerical value representing the median.
Assumes input values are typically in a reasonable range, e.g., for percentages.

common_confusions.label: Mean (Average)
common_confusions.difference: The mean is the sum of all values divided by the count, whereas the median is the middle value in a sorted list. The median is less affected by outliers than the mean.

common_confusions.label: Mode
common_confusions.difference: The mode is the most frequently occurring value in a dataset, not necessarily the middle value.

common_confusions.label: Range
common_confusions.difference: The range is the difference between the highest and lowest values, indicating data spread, not a measure of central tendency.

title: Taxable Income Calculation
summary: Calculates the final income amount subject to tax after applying above-the-line adjustments and selecting between standard or itemized deductions.
applicable_when: When determining the income base for calculating tax liability.
When comparing the impact of standard versus itemized deductions.
When analyzing the effect of various deductions on overall tax burden.
When preparing personal income tax returns.
not_applicable_when: When only gross income or adjusted gross income (AGI) is needed.
When calculating the final tax amount owed (before credits).
When only considering tax credits without prior income calculation.
scope_boundaries: Takes gross income, above-the-line deductions, standard deduction, and itemized deductions as inputs.
Calculates adjusted gross income (AGI) by subtracting above-the-line deductions from gross income.
Applies the greater of standard or itemized deductions to AGI.
The calculated taxable income can be negative if deductions exceed income.

common_confusions.label: Taxable Income vs. Gross Income
common_confusions.difference: Gross income is total income before any deductions; taxable income is the amount after all allowable deductions are applied.

common_confusions.label: Taxable Income vs. Adjusted Gross Income (AGI)
common_confusions.difference: AGI is gross income minus 'above-the-line' deductions. Taxable income is AGI minus the larger of standard or itemized 'below-the-line' deductions.

common_confusions.label: Taxable Income vs. Total Tax Due
common_confusions.difference: Taxable income is the amount on which tax rates are applied. Total tax due is the result of applying tax rates to taxable income, possibly adjusted by tax credits.

common_confusions.label: Standard Deduction vs. Itemized Deductions
common_confusions.difference: The standard deduction is a fixed amount based on filing status, while itemized deductions are specific allowable expenses (e.g., mortgage interest, medical expenses). The higher of the two is used.

common_confusions.label: Negative Taxable Income
common_confusions.difference: By default, taxable income can be negative if total deductions exceed income, reflecting a net loss. This card allows for a negative result unless explicitly floored at zero.

title: Long Put: Profit/Loss Calculation
summary: Calculates the profit or loss for a long put option strategy, considering strike price, premium paid, shares per option, and the final stock price.
applicable_when: You have purchased a put option.
You want to calculate the financial outcome of a long put option at expiration or exercise.
You are anticipating a decline in the underlying asset's price.
You are using a put option to hedge a long stock position against downside risk.
not_applicable_when: Calculating profit/loss for a short put option (selling a put).
Analyzing call options or other complex option strategies.
You are primarily interested in the profit/loss of a short stock position.
You are expecting the underlying asset price to rise significantly.
scope_boundaries: Focuses solely on the profit/loss of a long put option position.
Requires inputs for strike price, premium paid, shares per option, and final stock price.
Output is the total profit or loss from the single long put option position.

common_confusions.label: Long Put vs. Shorting Stock
common_confusions.difference: A long put offers limited risk (premium paid) and limited profit (stock price to zero), while shorting stock carries theoretically unlimited risk and limited profit. A long put requires the price to fall before expiration, whereas shorting stock profits from a price decline at any time.

common_confusions.label: Long Put (Speculative) vs. Protective Put (Hedging)
common_confusions.difference: A long put can be used for speculation (betting on a price drop) or hedging (protecting an existing long stock position, known as a protective put). This card calculates the general profit/loss, applicable to both uses.

Calculating investment interest deduction.
Calculating personal interest deduction (e.g., car loans, credit cards).

common_confusions.label: Home Equity Loan Use

title: Working Ratio
summary: Measures a company's ability to recover its operating costs from annual revenue. It is calculated by dividing adjusted total annual expenses (excluding depreciation and debt expenses) by the annual gross income. A ratio below one indicates that the company can cover its operating costs from its annual revenue, while a ratio above one indicates it cannot.
applicable_when: Assessing a company's operational efficiency.
Evaluating a company's ability to cover day-to-day operating expenses through annual gross income.
Analyzing a business's core financial viability regarding operational expenditures.
Determining how much annual revenue is consumed by operating costs.
not_applicable_when: When evaluating a company's overall profitability, as it excludes depreciation and debt expenses.
When assessing a company's ability to cover its debt obligations or interest expenses.
When a comprehensive view of financial health, including liquidity and solvency, is required.
When analyzing companies with highly volatile operating expenses without considering future projections or cash reserves.
scope_boundaries: Focuses strictly on operational expenses relative to annual gross income.
Excludes depreciation and debt-related expenses from the cost calculation.
Utilizes annual figures for both expenses and income.
Measures a specific aspect of financial sustainability related to operational cost recovery.

common_confusions.label: Overall Profitability
common_confusions.difference: The Working Ratio measures operational cost recovery, not overall profitability. It specifically excludes depreciation and debt expenses, which are vital for a complete profit assessment.

common_confusions.label: Debt Servicing Ability
common_confusions.difference: This ratio explicitly *excludes* debt expenses from its calculation, meaning it is not suitable for assessing a company's capacity to cover its debt obligations or interest payments.

common_confusions.label: Comprehensive Financial Health
common_confusions.difference: While an indicator of operational efficiency, the Working Ratio does not provide a holistic view of financial health, as it omits factors like liquidity, solvency, and non-operating income/expenses.

title: Basket of Goods
summary: Calculates a weighted price index for a fixed set of consumer goods and services. Primarily used for constructing measures like the Consumer Price Index (CPI) to track inflation and changes in the cost of living.
applicable_when: You need to measure inflation or changes in consumer prices over time.
You are constructing a Consumer Price Index (CPI).
You want to calculate the weighted average price of a specific set of items.
You are analyzing economic indicators related to household consumption costs.
not_applicable_when: You need to calculate a simple, unweighted average price.
You are analyzing price changes for producer goods or services (PPI).
You need to measure price changes for all domestically produced goods and services (GDP Deflator).
The composition or weights of the 'basket' items frequently change or are highly dynamic.
scope_boundaries: Requires a list of current prices for items.
Requires a corresponding list of non-negative weights for each item.
The sum of all provided weights must be approximately 1.
The number of prices must exactly match the number of weights.
All prices and weights must be non-negative numerical values.
The output is a single numerical weighted index value.

common_confusions.label: Inflation Rate Calculation
common_confusions.difference: This card computes the index value. To find the inflation rate, you must compare two index values from different periods to calculate the percentage change.

common_confusions.label: GDP Deflator
common_confusions.difference: The GDP deflator measures price changes for all goods and services produced domestically, while the 'Basket of Goods' focuses on a specific set of consumer items.

common_confusions.label: Producer Price Index (PPI)
common_confusions.difference: PPI measures average changes in selling prices received by domestic producers. This 'Basket of Goods' calculation is for consumer prices, similar to CPI.

common_confusions.label: Unweighted Average Price
common_confusions.difference: This formula specifically calculates a weighted index. It does not compute a simple arithmetic average of prices where all items contribute equally.

title: Quarter over Quarter (Q/Q)
summary: Calculates the Quarter-Over-Quarter (Q/Q) growth rate of a financial metric, such as earnings or revenue, expressed as a percentage. It measures the performance change between two consecutive fiscal quarters.
applicable_when: Analyzing short-term financial performance trends.
Evaluating growth or decline of metrics like revenue, earnings, or GDP between two consecutive quarters.
When quarterly financial statements or economic reports are available.
Assessing immediate business or economic changes.
not_applicable_when: When the previous quarter's value is zero, as this leads to an undefined growth rate.
For long-term trend analysis where Year-over-Year (YOY) might be more appropriate.
When comparing data across non-consecutive or non-aligned quarters (e.g., Q1 of Year 1 vs. Q3 of Year 2).
When input values are non-numeric or missing.
scope_boundaries: Focuses solely on the change between the immediately preceding quarter and the current quarter.
Calculates a growth rate as a percentage.
Applies to single financial or economic metrics.
Requires two consecutive quarterly data points for comparison.

common_confusions.label: Month over Month (M/M)
common_confusions.difference: Q/Q measures growth over a three-month period, making it less volatile and less susceptible to one-time events than M/M, which measures monthly changes.

common_confusions.label: Year over Year (YOY)
common_confusions.difference: Q/Q is more volatile than YOY. YOY compares the same quarter across different years, providing a smoother, longer-term picture by accounting for seasonality, which Q/Q does not inherently factor in.

common_confusions.label: Absolute Change
common_confusions.difference: Q/Q calculates a *percentage change* or growth rate, not an absolute numerical difference between the current and previous quarter's values.

title: Forward Premium
summary: Calculates the forward premium or discount as a percentage for a currency pair, with optional annualization.
applicable_when: You need to determine if a currency is trading at a premium or discount in the forward market.
You have a spot exchange rate and a forward exchange rate for a currency pair.
You want to quantify the short-term market expectation for a currency's movement.
You are analyzing the implications of interest rate differentials on currency forward rates.
not_applicable_when: You only have spot rates and no forward rates.
You are looking to calculate the outright forward exchange rate using interest rate parity.
You need to determine long-term fundamental currency valuation.
The currency pair or contract period is not clearly defined.
scope_boundaries: Focuses on the percentage difference between forward and spot exchange rates.
Supports optional annualization of the premium/discount.
By default, results are expressed as a percentage.
Default annualization assumes a 360-day year.
Interpretation of premium/discount is for the base currency based on direct quotation.

common_confusions.label: Forward Premium vs. Forward Discount
common_confusions.difference: A positive result indicates a forward premium (forward rate > spot rate), while a negative result indicates a forward discount (forward rate < spot rate).

common_confusions.label: Output as Decimal vs. Percentage
common_confusions.difference: The output is expressed as a percentage (e.g., 0.44%), not a decimal (e.g., 0.0044), by default.

common_confusions.label: Annualization Basis (360-day vs. 365-day year)
common_confusions.difference: Annualization uses a 360-day year (financial convention) by default, rather than a 365-day calendar year.

common_confusions.label: Interpretation for Base vs. Quote Currency
common_confusions.difference: The premium or discount is calculated and interpreted for the base currency based on the direct quotation of the currency pair.

title: Net Growth Rate (Customer/Subscriber Base)
summary: Calculates the net percentage change in a customer or subscriber base by subtracting the churn rate (lost subscribers) from the growth rate (new subscribers), relative to the total starting subscribers.
applicable_when: Analyzing the overall health and expansion of a subscription or customer-based business.
Evaluating whether customer acquisition efforts are successfully offsetting customer attrition.
Determining the net change in a company's customer or employee count over a specific period.
Operating in industries characterized by recurring customer relationships or high customer mobility (e.g., telecom, SaaS, streaming).
not_applicable_when: Only interested in the gross churn rate (percentage of lost customers) without considering new acquisitions.
Only interested in the gross growth rate (percentage of new customers) without considering losses.
For one-time transaction businesses where recurring customer relationships are not central to revenue.
When calculating metrics like Customer Lifetime Value (CLV) or Customer Acquisition Cost (CAC) directly.
scope_boundaries: Inputs: Requires specific counts for new subscribers acquired, subscribers lost, and total subscribers at the beginning of the period.
Output: Delivers a single percentage value representing the net change in the subscriber/customer base.
Reference Point: All rates are calculated relative to the total subscribers at the start of the period.
Timeframe: Applies to a defined reporting period (e.g., monthly, quarterly, annually).

common_confusions.label: Churn Rate
common_confusions.difference: This formula computes the net effect of growth and churn. A standalone churn rate only measures subscribers lost as a percentage of total, without factoring in new acquisitions.

common_confusions.label: Growth Rate
common_confusions.difference: This formula integrates growth with churn. A standalone growth rate typically only considers new subscribers as a percentage of total, without factoring in losses.

common_confusions.label: Customer Lifetime Value (CLV)
common_confusions.difference: While related to customer retention, CLV calculates the total revenue a business expects from a customer over their relationship, not the rate of change in the overall customer base.

title: Strangle
summary: Calculates the profit or loss for a long strangle options strategy. This strategy profits from significant price movements in the underlying asset in either direction, typically used when high volatility is expected but the direction of the price change is uncertain. It involves buying an out-of-the-money call and an out-of-the-money put option with the same expiration date.
applicable_when: Expecting significant price volatility in an underlying asset.
Uncertain about the direction of a large price movement.
Before major company events like earnings announcements.
During merger and acquisition rumors or announcements.
Ahead of regulatory decisions, such as FDA drug approvals, for biotech or pharmaceutical stocks.
not_applicable_when: When expecting minimal or stable price movement in the underlying asset.
When the direction of the price movement is confidently predicted.
When a precise, narrow price range is expected.
scope_boundaries: Focuses on a long strangle options strategy.
Requires the underlying asset's final price, call option strike price and premium, and put option strike price and premium.
Assumes the call option's strike price is higher than the put option's strike price, consistent with an out-of-the-money call and put.
Assumes both call and put options have the same expiration date.
Calculates the net profit or loss at expiration.

common_confusions.label: Profit/Loss Basis
common_confusions.difference: The calculation defaults to profit/loss before taxes. Clarification is needed if after-tax profit/loss is required.

common_confusions.label: Strangle vs. Straddle
common_confusions.difference: A strangle uses out-of-the-money call and put options, making it generally cheaper than a straddle, which uses at-the-money options. Both profit from volatility, but straddles typically require less significant price movement to become profitable due to their strike prices.

title: Weighted Average Cost of Capital (WACC)
summary: Calculates a company's average after-tax cost of capital from all funding sources (equity and debt), weighted by their market values. It represents the minimum return a company must earn on its existing asset base to satisfy its creditors and shareholders, often used as a discount rate or hurdle rate for investment evaluation.
applicable_when: To determine the average after-tax cost of a company's financing.
To evaluate the profitability and desirability of investment projects or acquisitions.
To use as a discount rate for future cash flows in valuation models.
To assess the overall risk and return expectations for a firm's capital.
not_applicable_when: If only the cost of equity or cost of debt for a specific component is required, not the blended cost.
If the project's risk profile deviates significantly from the company's average risk (a project-specific discount rate may be more appropriate).
If the company's capital structure is entirely equity or entirely debt, as simpler cost of capital calculations would apply.
For short-term financing decisions or evaluating individual asset financing.
scope_boundaries: Focuses on the total firm's capital structure, including common stock, preferred stock, bonds, and other forms of debt.
Requires market values for equity and debt, not book values, to accurately reflect current capital structure proportions.
Assumes costs of equity and debt, and the corporate tax rate, are provided as inputs.
Calculates an average cost across all capital sources, reflecting the overall cost of funding for the enterprise.

common_confusions.label: Cost of Equity (Re) or Cost of Debt (Rd)
common_confusions.difference: WACC is the blended, average cost of all capital sources (equity and debt), whereas Re and Rd are the costs of specific capital components in isolation.

common_confusions.label: Hurdle Rate or Discount Rate
common_confusions.difference: WACC is often *used* as a hurdle rate or discount rate for projects with similar risk to the company's average, but these terms are broader and can be adjusted for specific project risks or valuation contexts.

common_confusions.label: Book Value of Equity/Debt
common_confusions.difference: WACC calculations require the *market values* of equity and debt, not their book values, to accurately reflect current capital structure proportions.

common_confusions.label: Pre-tax Cost of Debt
common_confusions.difference: WACC uses the *after-tax* cost of debt, accounting for the tax deductibility of interest expenses, while the pre-tax cost is the raw borrowing rate before tax adjustments.

title: Purchasing Managers' Index (PMI)
summary: Calculates the Purchasing Managers' Index (PMI), a leading economic indicator based on survey responses of business conditions (improvement, no change, deterioration) in manufacturing and service sectors.
applicable_when: You need to assess current or future economic trends.
You are analyzing business conditions in manufacturing or service sectors.
You have survey data on business improvement, no change, or deterioration.
You want a leading indicator of economic activity.
You are evaluating market expansion or contraction.
not_applicable_when: You are seeking a company-specific financial performance metric.
You lack percentages for business improvement, no change, and deterioration.
You need a coincident or lagging economic indicator.
You are not interested in manufacturing or service sector trends.
scope_boundaries: Output is an index ranging from 0 to 100.
Input values are percentages representing survey responses.
Input percentages for improvement, no change, and deterioration must sum to 100%.
A PMI value above 50 indicates economic expansion.
A PMI value below 50 indicates economic contraction.
A PMI value of 50 indicates no change in conditions.

common_confusions.label: PMI Output Scale
common_confusions.difference: The PMI is conventionally reported as a value between 0 and 100, not scaled to a decimal between 0 and 1. For example, a PMI of 50 means 50.0, not 0.50.

title: Discounted Payback Period
summary: Calculates the time it takes for an investment's discounted cash inflows to cover its initial cost, considering the time value of money.
applicable_when: Evaluating the speed at which an investment repays its initial outlay
Comparing projects where early cash recovery is a priority
Assessing investment risk related to delayed returns
Considering the time value of money for project break-even analysis
not_applicable_when: Needing a measure of total project profitability (e.g., NPV, IRR)
Time value of money is explicitly ignored (use simple payback period)
Cash flows are not subject to discounting
scope_boundaries: Requires an initial investment and a series of future cash flows
Requires a discount rate for present value calculations
Output is in periods (e.g., years) until initial investment is recovered
Assumes periodic cash flows (e.g., annually, quarterly)

common_confusions.label: Simple Payback Period
common_confusions.difference: Simple Payback Period does not account for the time value of money, while Discounted Payback Period discounts future cash flows to their present value.

title: Margin Account Net Profit or Loss
summary: Calculates the net profit or loss from trading on margin, considering initial investment, borrowed funds, stock price changes, and interest expenses over a specified period.
applicable_when: Calculating the overall financial outcome of a margin trade.
An investor uses borrowed funds to buy securities.
Assessing performance of a leveraged investment impacted by stock price changes and interest.
Evaluating the monetary gain or loss from a margin position.
not_applicable_when: Only calculating interest paid on a loan without considering stock price changes.
Only calculating stock appreciation without considering borrowed funds or interest.
Trading in non-margin accounts (e.g., IRA, trust, or other fiduciary accounts).
Accounts with less than $2,000, which typically do not qualify for margin trading.
scope_boundaries: Focuses solely on the financial profit or loss of a single margin trade.
Includes initial investment, borrowed capital, stock price movement, and interest.
Assumes a single stock or a portfolio behaving as a single stock.
Does not account for taxes, commissions, or other trading fees beyond interest.
Output is an absolute monetary value of profit or loss.

common_confusions.label: Absolute vs. Percentage Return
common_confusions.difference: This card calculates the absolute monetary profit/loss. Users might expect a percentage return on initial investment, which is a different metric.

common_confusions.difference: Ambiguity regarding whether the 'interest_rate' is provided as an annual rate or a monthly rate.

title: Altman Z-Score
summary: Calculates the Altman Z-score to predict the bankruptcy risk of a publicly traded manufacturing company. This model uses a combination of profitability, leverage, liquidity, solvency, and activity ratios to assess financial distress.
applicable_when: Assessing the likelihood of corporate bankruptcy.
Evaluating the financial health of publicly traded manufacturing companies.
Analyzing credit strength based on a combination of financial ratios.
not_applicable_when: The company is private or non-manufacturing (consider Altman Z-score Plus for these cases).
Requiring an assessment for U.S. and non-U.S. companies (consider Altman Z-score Plus).
scope_boundaries: Requires specific financial data: working capital, total assets, retained earnings, EBIT, market value of equity, total liabilities, and sales.
Results apply to a single entity at a given financial reporting period.
Focuses on predicting corporate default likelihood.

common_confusions.label: Applicable company types
common_confusions.difference: The standard Altman Z-score is designed for publicly traded manufacturing companies. For private, non-manufacturing, or non-U.S. companies, the Altman Z-score Plus model is more appropriate.

common_confusions.label: Interpreting risk thresholds
common_confusions.difference: Interpretation thresholds vary. The original model suggests a score below 1.8 indicates bankruptcy risk and above 3 suggests stability. More recent interpretations suggest a score closer to 0 indicates financial trouble.

common_confusions.label: Output format
common_confusions.difference: The Z-score is typically expressed as a decimal value (e.g., 2.99). It can also be presented as a percentage if explicitly required, but this is less common.

title: Leverage Ratio (Debt-to-Capitalization)
summary: Evaluates a company's financial structure by calculating the proportion of its total debt relative to its total capitalization (debt plus equity).
applicable_when: Assessing a company's long-term solvency and financial risk.
Understanding how much of a company's assets are financed by debt.
Comparing capital structures of different companies.
Evaluating a company's reliance on debt versus equity.
not_applicable_when: Evaluating a company's ability to meet short-term obligations (use liquidity ratios).
Analyzing the efficiency of asset utilization (use asset turnover ratios).
Determining the impact of operating costs on income (use operating leverage).
When only interested in debt relative to equity (use Debt-to-Equity Ratio).
scope_boundaries: Considers all short-term and long-term debt.
Includes shareholders' equity as part of total capitalization.
Applies to corporate financial statements.
Calculates a ratio, not an absolute value.

common_confusions.label: Debt-to-Equity Ratio
common_confusions.difference: Measures total debt against shareholders' equity only. This card measures total debt against total capitalization (debt plus equity).

common_confusions.label: Debt-to-Asset Ratio
common_confusions.difference: Measures total debt against total assets. This card measures total debt against total capitalization (debt plus equity).

common_confusions.label: Interest Coverage Ratio
common_confusions.difference: Evaluates a company's ability to cover its interest payments with its earnings, rather than the proportion of debt in its capital structure.

common_confusions.label: Degree of Financial Leverage (DFL)
common_confusions.difference: Focuses on the sensitivity of earnings per share to changes in operating income, not a direct measure of capital structure or debt proportion.

title: Viager Transaction Value
summary: Calculates the total financial commitment for a buyer in a viager real estate transaction, encompassing the initial down payment and the sum of regular cash installments based on the seller's expected remaining lifespan.
applicable_when: When determining the total cost a buyer will pay in a viager property agreement.
When an initial down payment (bouquet) and ongoing regular cash installments are specified.
When the seller retains the right to live in the property for life.
When an estimate of the seller's remaining life is available.
not_applicable_when: For standard real estate purchases with immediate full ownership transfer and no life annuity.
When calculating the full market value of a property without considering the viager structure.
When the user is a seller trying to determine their total revenue from a viager.
When analyzing a reverse mortgage or a charitable remainder trust, which have different structures.
scope_boundaries: Requires the occupied value of the property.
Requires the percentage of the down payment.
Requires the seller's life expectancy in years.
Requires the annual cash installment amount.
Output represents the total cost from the buyer's perspective.

common_confusions.label: Down Payment Percentage Scale
common_confusions.difference: Distinguishing between a whole number (e.g., 25 for 25%) and a decimal (e.g., 0.25) for the down payment percentage.

common_confusions.label: Cash Installment Frequency
common_confusions.difference: Clarifying if cash installments are provided annually or monthly.

common_confusions.label: Transaction Perspective
common_confusions.difference: Understanding if the calculation is for the buyer's total cost or the seller's total revenue.

common_confusions.label: Life Expectancy Basis
common_confusions.difference: Distinguishing between life expectancy based on average demographic data versus actuarial calculations.

common_confusions.label: Viager vs. Standard Property Sale
common_confusions.difference: This formula is specific to viager transactions, not typical property sales with immediate full possession and market value pricing.

Assessing performance of investments and portfolios

Comparing investment or portfolio performance to benchmarks or peers
Analyzing trends in financial performance over a partial year
Projecting end-of-year financial outcomes
When you have initial and current investment values, and the number of months passed
not_applicable_when: When seeking a simple YTD return without annualization
When the investment period is exactly one full year or multiple full years
When calculating YTD earnings or net pay (this card is for investment yield)
When data points span across multiple calendar or fiscal years
When only total return over an arbitrary period is needed

Requires initial and current investment values, and months passed within the year
Yield is annualized to a 12-month period

applicable_when: Determining the profit or loss of a call butterfly options strategy.
Evaluating returns for a three-strike options spread.
Analyzing an options position at its expiration.
Calculating the outcome of a long or short call butterfly spread.
not_applicable_when: Calculating profit or loss for put butterfly spreads, as different logic is required.
Analyzing options strategies other than call butterfly spreads (e.g., straddles, iron condors).
Estimating profit or loss before the option's expiration date.
When options are not call options.
scope_boundaries: Applies exclusively to call butterfly spreads.
Requires three equidistant strike prices (lower, middle, upper).
Calculates profit/loss at the specific expiration date.
Accounts for both long and short call butterfly positions.
Assumes the premium cost includes all transaction fees if applicable.

title: Weighted Average
summary: Calculates the weighted average of a set of values, such as investment returns, by assigning relative importance (weights) to each component. This ensures that more impactful data points contribute proportionally more to the final average.
applicable_when: You need to account for the varying importance or size of individual components when calculating an average.
Analyzing portfolio returns where different assets have different allocations.
Calculating average costs where quantities or prices vary.
Evaluating metrics like Weighted Average Cost of Capital (WACC) or weighted moving averages.
When a simple average would misrepresent the true central tendency due to unequal contributions.
not_applicable_when: All values in a dataset have equal importance or weight.
A simple arithmetic average is sufficient for analysis.
Weights or proportions for each value are not available or cannot be determined meaningfully.
You need to calculate a median, mode, or geometric average.
scope_boundaries: Requires a list of numerical values and a corresponding list of numerical weights.
The lists of values and weights must be of the same length.
Weights cannot be empty, and their sum must not be zero to avoid division by zero.
Output is a single numerical value representing the weighted average, typically in percentage terms by default.

common_confusions.label: Simple Average (Arithmetic Mean)
common_confusions.difference: A simple average treats all values equally, whereas a weighted average assigns different levels of importance (weights) to each value, making some contribute more to the final average.

common_confusions.label: Index Weighting (e.g., S&P 500 vs. DJIA)
common_confusions.difference: Index weighting refers to how components of a market index are combined (e.g., market-cap weighted or price-weighted). This formula calculates a weighted average of specific input values and their corresponding weights, not an index value.

common_confusions.label: Time-Weighted Average Rate of Return
common_confusions.difference: Time-Weighted Average Rate of Return is a specific methodology for measuring investment performance, typically removing the effects of cash flows. This formula calculates a general weighted average of given values and weights, which might be a component of such a calculation but isn't the full TWRR itself.

title: Adjusted Present Value (APV)
summary: Calculates the Adjusted Present Value (APV) of a firm or project. APV separates a firm's value into its unlevered, all-equity value and the present value of debt financing effects, specifically the tax shield from interest payments.
selection_hints: Determine project value with debt financing
Assess firm value considering tax shields
Value a company with a changing capital structure
Analyze impact of debt on firm valuation
Need to value unlevered firm and add financing benefits
applicable_when: The capital structure is expected to change significantly over time.
Evaluating projects or firms involved in leveraged buyouts (LBOs).
Assessing major corporate restructurings.
Evaluating firms with intricate debt arrangements.
Calculating the present value of the tax shield from debt.
Separating operational value from financing effects.
not_applicable_when: The Weighted Average Cost of Capital (WACC) is constant and easily determinable.
Other financing effects beyond tax shields are significant and require explicit modeling within this calculation.
A single discount rate can accurately reflect the firm's overall risk and financing structure throughout the projection period.
scope_boundaries: Calculates the total value as the sum of the unlevered firm value and the present value of the debt tax shield.
Includes the tax benefits arising from the deductibility of interest payments on debt.
Does not explicitly model other financing effects such as costs of financial distress or subsidies.
Assumes interest rate and tax rate are constant over the specified periods for tax shield calculation.

common_confusions.label: Adjusted Present Value (APV) vs. Net Present Value (NPV)
common_confusions.difference: APV starts with the unlevered NPV and adds the present value of financing side effects (like tax shields). Traditional NPV (often using WACC) incorporates financing effects directly into the discount rate.

common_confusions.label: Adjusted Present Value (APV) vs. Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) with WACC
common_confusions.difference: APV is more suitable when the capital structure is expected to change significantly or when financing effects are complex and best valued separately. DCF with WACC assumes a constant target capital structure and a single WACC that accounts for financing effects.

title: Kijun-Sen (Base Line)
summary: Calculates the Kijun-sen, or Base Line, a core component of the Ichimoku Kinko Hyo technical analysis system. It identifies the midpoint of the highest and lowest prices over a specified period to gauge short- to medium-term trend direction and momentum.
applicable_when: Assessing short- to medium-term price trends.
Identifying price momentum (up or down).
Using Ichimoku Kinko Hyo for technical analysis.
Looking for potential trading signals (e.g., with Tenkan-sen).
Analyzing price equilibrium or midpoint levels.
not_applicable_when: Seeking a simple average of closing prices (e.g., Simple Moving Average).
During prolonged range-bound or sideways markets.
Requiring only very short-term or very long-term trend insights without period adjustment.
Analyzing financial instruments without distinct high and low prices.
scope_boundaries: Operates on historical high and low price data.
Output is a single price value representing the midpoint.
Calculation is dependent on a user-defined lookback period.
Provides insight into a specific historical window.

common_confusions.label: Simple Moving Average (SMA)
common_confusions.difference: Kijun-sen calculates the midpoint of the highest and lowest prices over a period, whereas an SMA averages closing prices. They provide different types of market insights.

title: Fixed Asset Turnover Ratio
summary: Measures a company's efficiency in generating net sales from its fixed asset investments, calculated by dividing net sales by average fixed assets.
applicable_when: Analyzing how efficiently a company uses its fixed assets to generate sales.
Evaluating capital-intensive businesses or industries with significant Property, Plant, and Equipment (PP&E).
Assessing operational performance related to fixed asset investments.
Comparing asset utilization efficiency among peer companies within the same industry.
not_applicable_when: Comparing companies across industries with vastly different capital structures (e.g., technology vs. manufacturing).
Evaluating companies with minimal fixed assets where other asset turnover metrics might be more relevant.
Assessing overall profitability or cash flow, as it only considers sales and fixed assets.
Analyzing companies with highly cyclical sales without considering multi-period trends.
scope_boundaries: Considers Net Sales as the revenue generated.
Focuses solely on Fixed Assets (e.g., PP&E) as the investment base.
Uses the average balance of fixed assets over a period.
Measures efficiency in converting fixed assets into sales.

common_confusions.label: Asset Turnover Ratio
common_confusions.difference: Fixed Asset Turnover (FAT) uses only fixed assets in its calculation, highlighting efficiency from long-term productive assets. The Asset Turnover Ratio uses total assets, reflecting the efficiency of all assets (current and fixed) in generating sales.

title: Deferred Interest Loan Balance
summary: Calculates the loan balance after a period where unpaid interest is added to the principal, resulting in deferred interest or negative amortization.
applicable_when: Calculating the future balance of a loan with deferred interest.
When minimum payments do not cover the full accrued interest.
Evaluating loans where unpaid interest is capitalized (added to the principal).
Analyzing mortgages with negative amortization features.
Understanding the impact of promotional loan periods with interest deferral.
not_applicable_when: When interest is always fully paid each period.
Calculating simple interest without capitalization.
Determining the balance of a standard amortizing loan.
Calculating interest where it is retroactively applied to the original balance if a promotional period is not met (credit card style).
When payments exceed the monthly interest accrual, preventing deferred interest.
scope_boundaries: The calculation assumes unpaid interest is added to the current principal balance (negative amortization style).
The output is the updated loan balance after the specified number of months.

common_confusions.label: Deferred Interest (Negative Amortization) vs. Retroactive Interest (Credit Card Style)
common_confusions.difference: This card calculates deferred interest by adding unpaid interest to the current principal balance. It does NOT calculate interest that is retroactively applied to the original balance if a promotional period's conditions (e.g., paying off the full balance) are not met, which is common in some credit card offers.

title: Event Study
summary: This tool calculates the cumulative abnormal returns for a security over an event window. It is used in event studies to assess the quantifiable impact of a specific event or occurrence on stock performance by comparing actual returns to expected returns.
applicable_when: Analyzing the impact of a specific corporate announcement (e.g., earnings, M&A, bankruptcy) on stock prices.
Evaluating the market reaction to regulatory changes or policy decisions affecting a security.
Quantifying the change in security value due to a discrete event.
When comparing actual stock returns against a benchmark of expected returns around a particular date.
Determining the abnormal return generated by an event.
not_applicable_when: When the goal is general stock valuation or intrinsic value calculation unrelated to specific events.
For direct forecasting of future stock prices without an event context.
If historical actual returns or a reliable method for expected returns are unavailable.
For broad portfolio performance evaluation not tied to discrete events.
When analyzing long-term fundamental shifts rather than short-term event impacts.
scope_boundaries: Requires a defined event window for analysis.
Inputs for actual and expected returns must be provided as decimal values.
Assumes the market model or chosen benchmark accurately represents normal returns in the absence of the event.
The lists of actual and expected returns must have the same length.
The output is the cumulative abnormal return, expressed as a percentage.

common_confusions.label: General Stock Performance Analysis
common_confusions.difference: Event Study specifically focuses on isolating and quantifying the impact of a discrete event on stock returns. General performance analysis typically assesses overall returns or portfolio performance without isolating specific event effects.

common_confusions.label: Total Returns
common_confusions.difference: Total Returns represent the overall returns earned by an investment. Abnormal Returns, which are the basis for this calculation, measure the difference between actual returns and expected returns, thereby isolating the event-specific impact from market or industry movements.

common_confusions.label: Average Abnormal Returns (AAR)
common_confusions.difference: This tool calculates Cumulative Abnormal Returns (CAR), which is the sum of abnormal returns over an event window for a single security. AAR typically refers to the average abnormal return across multiple securities or events, often used in cross-sectional studies.

title: Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) Valuation
summary: Calculates the present value of a series of future cash flows by discounting them back to the present using a specified discount rate. This is a core method for investment and asset valuation.
applicable_when: Evaluating the intrinsic value of an asset or project.
Comparing investment opportunities with known future cash flows.
Performing long-term financial analysis for investments.
When a reliable discount rate can be determined.
not_applicable_when: Cash flows are highly uncertain or cannot be reliably estimated.
Only a relative valuation (e.g., comparison to similar companies) is needed.
The appropriate discount rate is unknown or cannot be determined.
The timing of cash flows (beginning vs. end of period) is critical and ambiguous.
Short-term analysis where market value might be more relevant than intrinsic value.
scope_boundaries: Requires a list of future cash flows (positive for inflows, negative for outflows).
Requires a single discount rate, which should be expressed as a decimal.
Assumes cash flows occur at the end of each period by default.
The discount rate must be greater than -1.

common_confusions.label: Discount Rate Unit
common_confusions.difference: The formula expects the discount rate as a decimal (e.g., 0.08 for 8%). If you have a percentage (e.g., 8%), convert it to decimal before input.

common_confusions.label: Cash Flow Timing
common_confusions.difference: This calculation assumes cash flows occur at the end of each period (ordinary annuity). For beginning-of-period (annuity-due) cash flows, the present value would be higher and requires an adjustment.

common_confusions.label: DCF vs. NPV
common_confusions.difference: This formula calculates the present value of a stream of cash flows (DCF). Net Present Value (NPV) typically subtracts an initial investment (often at time zero) from the DCF.

If you have a percentage (e.g., 8%), convert it to decimal before input. Cash Flow Timing This calculation assumes cash flows occur at the end of each period (ordinary annuity).
